gk.t: India has 28 states and 8 union territories
gk.teaser: States have their own governments; UTs are run by the centre.
gk.more: The newest state is Telangana (2014, carved from Andhra Pradesh). Delhi and Puducherry are special UTs with their own assemblies. Jammu & Kashmir became a UT in 2019. Largest state by area: Rajasthan; by population: Uttar Pradesh (~240 million — more people than most countries).

gk.teaser: Jana Gana Mana, written by Rabindranath Tagore.
gk.more: Tagore wrote it in Bengali in 1911; it was adopted as the anthem in 1950. He also wrote Bangladesh's anthem — the only person whose songs are two countries' anthems. Vande Mataram (by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee) is the national song, which has equal status but no time rule.

gk.teaser: Asia is the largest continent; the Pacific the largest ocean.
gk.more: Order by size: Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Antarctica, Europe, Australia. The five oceans: Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, Arctic. The Pacific alone is bigger than all land on Earth combined.

gk.t: Light takes ~8 minutes from the Sun to Earth
gk.teaser: When you see the Sun, you see it 8 minutes in the past.
gk.more: Light travels ~300,000 km per second; the Sun is ~150 million km away. From the Moon, light takes just 1.3 seconds. The nearest star beyond the Sun, Proxima Centauri, is 4.2 light YEARS away — meaning you see it as it was 4 years ago.

gk.t: The Indian rupee symbol ₹ arrived only in 2010
gk.teaser: Designed by D. Udaya Kumar, blending Devanagari 'Ra' and Latin 'R'.
gk.more: Before 2010 India wrote 'Rs.' like several other countries. The horizontal lines represent the tricolour and the equality sign — a nod to economic balance. Only a handful of currencies have unique symbols: $, €, £, ¥, ₹.

gk.t: Mount Everest grows ~4 mm every year
gk.teaser: At 8,849 m it's the world's highest point — and still rising.
gk.more: The India-Eurasia plate collision keeps pushing it up. Its official height was revised in 2020 jointly by Nepal and China. The first confirmed summit: Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay, 29 May 1953.

gk.t: India's independence and republic days differ by purpose
gk.teaser: 15 Aug 1947 = freedom; 26 Jan 1950 = our own Constitution.
gk.more: Independence Day marks the end of British rule. Republic Day marks the Constitution coming into force, making India a republic with its own head of state instead of the British monarch. That's why Republic Day has the grand parade — it celebrates the state India built for itself.

gk.t: A cheque and a demand draft are not the same
gk.teaser: A cheque can bounce; a DD is prepaid so it cannot.
gk.more: A cheque instructs YOUR bank to pay and fails if your balance is short. A demand draft is bought from the bank upfront — the bank itself guarantees payment. That's why institutions ask for DDs for fees. Both are fading as UPI handles 80%+ of India's retail digital payments.

gk.t: The Olympics happen every 4 years — mostly
gk.teaser: Summer and Winter Games alternate every 2 years.
gk.more: Cancelled only by the World Wars (1916, 1940, 1944) and delayed once by COVID (Tokyo 2020 held in 2021). India's first Olympic medal as an independent nation: hockey gold, 1948 London — beating Britain in Britain a year after independence.

gk.t: Why a year has 365 days and a leap year 366
gk.teaser: Earth's orbit takes 365.25 days — the calendar catches up every 4 years.
gk.more: The extra quarter-day is banked and added as Feb 29. Century years skip it unless divisible by 400 (2000 yes, 2100 no) because the true figure is 365.2422, not exactly 365.25. Without this, seasons would drift through the calendar.

gk.t: The seven wonders include the Taj Mahal
gk.teaser: Chosen by a global vote of 100 million people in 2007.
gk.more: The 'New 7 Wonders': Taj Mahal, Great Wall of China, Petra (Jordan), Christ the Redeemer (Brazil), Machu Picchu (Peru), Chichén Itzá (Mexico), and the Colosseum (Rome). Of the original ancient wonders, only the Great Pyramid of Giza still stands.

gk.teaser: Simple pays on the principal; compound pays on interest too.
gk.more: ₹1 lakh at 10% simple = ₹10,000 every year, flat. Compound = ₹10,000 the first year, ₹11,000 the next, and so on — because interest joins the principal. Fixed deposits compound quarterly, which is why their 'effective yield' is a bit higher than the headline rate.

gk.teaser: Listed in the Constitution's Eighth Schedule — Hindi isn't the 'national' language.
gk.more: Hindi is an official language of the Union alongside English; no language is legally 'national'. The 22 include Bengali, Telugu, Marathi, Tamil, Urdu, Gujarati and Sanskrit. India has 120+ languages with 10,000+ speakers each — the world's most multilingual large country.

gk.t: Why the sea is salty but rivers are not
gk.teaser: Rivers carry tiny amounts of salt to the sea — and the sea never drains.
gk.more: Rainwater dissolves minute quantities of minerals from rocks into rivers. Rivers flow to the sea, where water evaporates but salt stays behind, concentrating over billions of years. Rivers taste fresh only because their salt keeps moving on instead of accumulating.

gk.t: ISRO launched 104 satellites in one rocket
gk.teaser: A world record in 2017, from a single PSLV flight.
gk.more: Most were tiny foreign 'nanosatellites' — proof of ISRO's reputation as the world's budget-friendly, reliable launcher. India was also first to reach Mars orbit on its first attempt (Mangalyaan, 2014) — at a cost less than the movie Gravity.

gk.t: India's Constitution is the world's longest
gk.teaser: Adopted 26 Nov 1949, in force 26 Jan 1950.
gk.more: B.R. Ambedkar chaired the drafting committee. It borrowed deliberately: parliamentary system from Britain, fundamental rights from the US, directive principles from Ireland, emergency provisions from Germany. Jan 26 was chosen because Congress declared 'Purna Swaraj' on that date in 1930.

gk.teaser: India's central bank predates independence by 12 years.
gk.more: Set up under the RBI Act 1934 based on the Hilton Young Commission, it was privately owned until nationalisation in 1949. It issues currency, sets the repo rate, regulates banks, and manages the rupee — its first governor was Osborne Smith; C.D. Deshmukh was the first Indian governor.

gk.teaser: A balance-of-payments crisis forced liberalisation.
gk.more: With forex reserves down to weeks of imports, PM Narasimha Rao and FM Manmohan Singh dismantled the licence raj, devalued the rupee, cut tariffs and opened doors to foreign investment. Nearly every big private-sector story since — IT, telecom, startups — traces to this pivot.

gk.teaser: Land heats faster than sea — wind follows.
gk.more: By June the subcontinent's landmass is much hotter than the Indian Ocean, creating a low-pressure zone that pulls moisture-laden winds northeast. They break on the Western Ghats and Himalayas as rain. About 70% of India's annual rainfall arrives in these four months, which is why a 'bad monsoon' moves inflation, farm incomes and even elections.

gk.t: Ashoka: the emperor who renounced war
gk.teaser: His wheel sits at the centre of the tricolour.
gk.more: After the bloody Kalinga war (~261 BCE), Mauryan emperor Ashoka embraced Buddhism and spread edicts on rock pillars across the subcontinent. The Ashoka Chakra on India's flag and the Lion Capital national emblem both come from his pillars at Sarnath.

gk.teaser: 1914–18 and 1939–45 — the second killed ~70–85 million.
gk.more: WWI started with an assassination in Sarajevo and ended empires (Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, Russian Tsar). WWII began with Germany invading Poland and ended with atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Over 2.5 million Indian soldiers served in WWII — the largest volunteer army in history.

gk.teaser: Founded 1945 by 51 countries so WWIII never happens.
gk.more: Headquartered in New York, its Security Council has five veto-holding permanent members: US, UK, France, Russia, China. India is a founding member and the largest cumulative contributor of UN peacekeeping troops, but its long push for a permanent Security Council seat remains unresolved.

gk.teaser: One night in November ended Europe's clearest Cold War symbol.
gk.more: Built in 1961 to stop East Germans fleeing to the capitalist West, the wall cut Berlin in two for 28 years. Its fall on 9 November 1989 triggered German reunification within a year and previewed the Soviet collapse of 1991.

gk.teaser: A military pact where attacking one member means attacking all.
gk.more: Founded 1949, the North Atlantic Treaty Organization's Article 5 collective-defence clause has been invoked once — after 9/11. It expanded eastward after the Cold War, which Russia cites among grievances behind the Ukraine conflict. India is not a member and stays formally non-aligned.

gk.teaser: 27 countries, one market, 20 sharing the euro.
gk.more: Born from post-WWII efforts to bind France and Germany economically so they'd never fight again, the European Union lets goods, services, money and people move freely across members. Brexit (2020) made the UK the only country ever to leave.

gk.t: Why there's an International Date Line
gk.teaser: Cross it westward and you skip a day.
gk.more: The Earth has 24 time zones; somewhere the calendar must flip. The line zigzags through the Pacific to avoid splitting countries. Fly Delhi to San Francisco and you can land at an earlier local time than you took off — the date line gives the day back.

gk.teaser: It replaced a maze of central and state levies.
gk.more: The Goods and Services Tax merged excise, VAT, service tax and more into one destination-based tax with slabs (0/5/12/18/28%). It made interstate commerce dramatically simpler — trucks no longer queue at state borders for tax checks — and every invoice feeds a single national IT backbone.

gk.teaser: In 2023 India became the first to land near the lunar south pole.
gk.more: After Chandrayaan-2's lander crashed in 2019, ISRO's Vikram lander touched down softly on 23 August 2023 — making India the fourth country to soft-land on the Moon and the first at the south polar region, where ice deposits could support future missions. Mission cost: roughly ₹615 crore, less than many Hollywood films.

gk.teaser: The first message in 1969 was meant to be 'LOGIN' — it crashed at 'LO'.
gk.more: A US defence research network connecting four universities grew into the internet after TCP/IP became its common language in 1983. The web (websites, URLs, browsers) came later — invented by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989 as a layer on top of the internet.

gk.teaser: Chip transistor counts doubled roughly every two years for 50 years.
gk.more: Intel co-founder Gordon Moore's 1965 observation became the tech industry's metronome — it's why phones outpower old supercomputers. Physical limits have slowed it, which is partly why the industry pivoted to specialised chips (GPUs, AI accelerators) instead of just faster general processors.

gk.teaser: The chip element, not the software.
gk.more: The Santa Clara Valley earned the name in the 1970s from silicon semiconductor makers like Fairchild and Intel. Software, internet and AI giants came in later waves. Bengaluru earned the 'India's Silicon Valley' tag through the 1990s IT services boom — Infosys and Wipro, not chips.

gk.teaser: A harmless preview teaches your immune system the enemy's face.
gk.more: Vaccines present a dead, weakened, or fragment version of a pathogen (or, in mRNA vaccines, instructions to build one protein of it), so your body develops antibodies without the disease. Smallpox — which killed ~300 million people in the 20th century alone — became the first disease ever eradicated, in 1980.

gk.teaser: India is still crashing into Asia at ~5 cm a year.
gk.more: Around 50 million years ago the Indian plate slammed into the Eurasian plate; the crumple zone is the Himalayas, still rising a few millimetres yearly. The same collision makes the region earthquake-prone — the reason Delhi sits in seismic zone IV.

gk.teaser: Air scatters blue light more than red.
gk.more: Sunlight contains all colours; molecules in the atmosphere scatter shorter (bluer) wavelengths far more — called Rayleigh scattering. At sunset, light travels through more air, the blue scatters away before reaching you, leaving reds and oranges.

gk.teaser: Gravity so strong that light can't leave.
gk.more: When a massive star collapses, its gravity can warp space so severely nothing escapes past the 'event horizon'. They're detected by their effect on nearby matter. The first-ever photo of one (M87*, 2019) took a telescope network the size of Earth.

gk.teaser: It saves ships ~7,000 km between Europe and Asia.
gk.more: Opened in 1869 through Egypt, it carries about 12% of global trade. When one ship (Ever Given) wedged sideways for six days in 2021, it blocked ~$10 billion of goods per day — a live demo of how fragile supply chains are.

gk.teaser: 1526 Panipat → 1757 Plassey → 1857 Revolt → 1947.
gk.more: Babur founded Mughal rule in 1526; it peaked under Akbar and Aurangzeb. The East India Company's victory at Plassey (1757) began company rule; after the 1857 revolt, the British Crown took direct control ('the Raj') until independence in 1947.

gk.teaser: India's Cold War stance: neither US bloc nor Soviet bloc.
gk.more: Co-founded by Nehru, Egypt's Nasser and Yugoslavia's Tito in 1961, NAM let newly independent nations avoid picking sides. In practice India tilted toward Soviet defence supplies after the 1962 China war and 1971 US support for Pakistan.

gk.teaser: ~10–15 million displaced; up to a million dead.
gk.more: British India split into India and Pakistan along the hastily drawn Radcliffe Line, published two days after independence. It remains one of history's largest forced migrations and the root of the Kashmir dispute and three India-Pakistan wars (1947, 1965, 1971 — the last creating Bangladesh).

gk.teaser: Earth's year is 365.2422 days — the calendar has to catch up.
gk.more: Adding Feb 29 every four years overcorrects slightly, so century years skip it unless divisible by 400 (2000 was a leap year; 2100 won't be). Without leap years, seasons would drift — in ~700 years, June would be winter in Delhi.

gk.teaser: You don't vote for President — MPs and MLAs do.
gk.more: India's President is elected by an electoral college of elected MPs and state MLAs, with votes weighted by population. Real executive power sits with the PM and council of ministers; the President's key discretionary moments come in hung parliaments and constitutional crises.

gk.teaser: Two straight quarters of shrinking GDP is the common shorthand.
gk.more: Economists actually judge by depth, duration and breadth of decline across jobs, income, output and sales. India's COVID quarter (Apr–Jun 2020, GDP −24%) was its sharpest recorded contraction; the 2008 global crisis, by contrast, only slowed India's growth without shrinking it.

gk.teaser: Ancient games ran ~776 BCE to 393 CE; revived 1896.
gk.more: The ancient Greek games honoured Zeus at Olympia until a Roman emperor banned pagan festivals. Frenchman Pierre de Coubertin revived them in Athens in 1896. India's first individual gold came only in 2008 — Abhinav Bindra, shooting.

gk.t: Why DNA is called a blueprint
gk.teaser: Four letters — A, T, G, C — spell out every living thing.
gk.more: DNA is a molecule storing instructions to build proteins; genes are its meaningful sentences. Humans share ~99.9% of DNA with each other and ~60% with bananas. The 2003 Human Genome Project read all 3 billion letters; today a genome can be sequenced in hours.

gk.teaser: Money earning money on the money it already earned.
gk.more: ₹1 lakh at 12% becomes ₹3.1 lakh in 10 years and ₹9.6 lakh in 20 — the growth accelerates because each year's interest joins the principal. The 'rule of 72': divide 72 by the interest rate to estimate doubling time. At 12%, money doubles every ~6 years.

learn.title: P/E ratio: the market's price tag
learn.teaser: How many years of profit you're paying for one share.
body: Price-to-Earnings = share price ÷ earnings per share. A P/E of 25 means you pay ₹25 for every ₹1 of annual profit. High P/E signals the market expects fast growth (or the stock is expensive); low P/E signals slow growth expectations (or a bargain).

It's only meaningful against context: the company's own history, its sector average, and growth rate. Indian IT often trades at 20–30; PSU banks far lower. A 'cheap' stock with shrinking profits is usually cheap for a reason.

learn.title: Correlation is not causation
learn.teaser: The most important sentence in analytics.
body: Ice cream sales and drownings rise together — summer causes both. When two metrics move together, there are always four possibilities: A causes B, B causes A, something else causes both, or coincidence. Dashboards show correlation; only controlled experiments or careful causal analysis show causation.
Practical BI reflex: before reporting 'X drove Y', ask what else changed in the same window — a campaign, a price change, seasonality, a tracking change. The last one bites analysts most often.

learn.title: Mean vs median: which lies less?
learn.teaser: One billionaire in a room makes the 'average' person rich.
body: The mean (sum ÷ count) is dragged by outliers; the median (middle value) isn't. Average income sounds impressive when a few earn crores; median income tells you what the typical person makes. Same for ticket sizes, session times, order values.

Rule of thumb: for skewed data (money, wait times, engagement), report the median or percentiles. If mean and median differ a lot, that gap itself is a finding — your distribution has a long tail worth investigating.

learn.title: Opportunity cost
learn.teaser: The real cost of anything is what you gave up to get it.
body: Spending ₹15 lakh on a car isn't just ₹15 lakh — it's also the ~₹47 lakh that money could become in 10 years at 12%. Every choice of time or money silently declines the alternatives. Economists call the best foregone alternative the opportunity cost.

It reframes decisions: a 'free' three-hour meeting costs the work you'd have shipped. An okay job offer costs the better one you stopped searching for. Comparing against 'doing nothing' is almost always the wrong baseline.

learn.title: How IPOs actually work
learn.teaser: A private company sells shares to the public for the first time.
body: The company hires investment banks to set a price band, files a prospectus (DRHP) with SEBI, and collects bids. Retail investors apply in lots; oversubscribed issues allot by lottery. On listing day the stock starts trading — sometimes far above or below issue price.

Listing 'pops' make headlines, but the base rate is sobering: many hyped IPOs trade below issue price within a year (India's 2021 tech-IPO batch is the classic case). Reading where the money goes — fresh growth capital vs early investors cashing out — tells you a lot.

learn.title: A/B testing in five sentences
learn.teaser: Split users randomly, change one thing, measure the difference.
body: Random assignment makes the two groups statistically identical except for your change, so any outcome gap is caused by it — this is the cleanest causation tool in product work. The catch is sample size: small tests 'win' by luck constantly.

Standard practice: decide the metric and minimum effect before launching, run a significance check (p < 0.05 convention), and beware peeking early — checking daily and stopping at the first 'significant' result inflates false positives dramatically.

learn.title: Why the Sensex is called the Sensex
learn.teaser: 'Sensitive Index' — 30 big BSE stocks standing in for the market.
body: It tracks 30 of the largest, most-traded companies on the Bombay Stock Exchange, Asia's oldest (1875). Base value: 100 in 1978–79 — so ~77,000 means those stocks are worth roughly 770× their 1979 level. The Nifty 50 is the NSE's 50-stock equivalent.
Index composition changes over time — laggards get dropped, risers added — which builds in survivorship: the index isn't the same 30 companies your grandfather's Sensex had.

learn.title: What the repo rate actually does
learn.teaser: RBI's lending rate to banks — the master dial for every EMI.
body: Banks borrow from RBI at the repo rate. Raise it and all credit gets costlier, cooling spending and inflation; cut it and money cheapens, boosting growth. The Monetary Policy Committee resets it every two months against a 4% (±2%) CPI target.

Transmission is the catch: banks pass rate cuts to depositors fast and to borrowers slowly. That lag — and how much of a cut actually reaches your home loan — is what analysts fight about after every MPC meeting.

learn.title: What GDP measures — and misses
learn.teaser: Everything produced in a year; nothing about who gets it.
body: GDP is the market value of final goods and services produced in a period. India's ~$4 trillion ranks among the world's largest — but per capita it ranks far lower, divided across 1.4 billion people.
It ignores unpaid work, inequality and environmental damage, and a hospital bill raises GDP the same as a festival. '7% growth' means output grew — not that everyone is 7% better off.

learn.title: How India measures inflation
learn.teaser: CPI prices a fixed basket a typical household buys.
body: Food is nearly half of India's CPI basket, so vegetable prices swing the headline number. '6% inflation' means the basket costs 6% more than a year ago. RBI's legal mandate: keep it at 4%, within 2–6%.

WPI tracks wholesale prices businesses pay each other; the two can diverge sharply. For personal finance, your true inflation depends on your basket — education and health have outpaced CPI for years.

learn.title: What an LLM actually is
learn.teaser: A next-word predictor trained on most of the written internet.
body: Models like GPT, Gemini and Claude predict the next token; at sufficient scale that absorbs grammar, facts, reasoning patterns and code. Chat behaviour comes from fine-tuning with human feedback afterward.

Two limits follow: hallucination (fluent but wrong — they optimise plausibility, not truth) and frozen knowledge (they know training data, not today) unless given search or documents. Every 'AI made this up' story traces to the first; every 'AI doesn't know X' to the second.

learn.title: Why oil moves everything
learn.teaser: A fifth of world supply ships through one strait near Iran.
body: Crude feeds transport, plastics, fertiliser and power — an oil spike is a tax on the whole economy. The Strait of Hormuz chokepoint is why Gulf tensions instantly lift Brent.

India imports 85%+ of its crude: costlier oil widens the trade deficit, weakens the rupee, and lifts CPI. That chain — Gulf headline → rupee → your petrol pump → RBI's next rate call — is worth internalising.

learn.title: Bulls, bears, and the names
learn.teaser: Horns toss up; paws swipe down.
body: Bull market: sustained rise (+20% from a low). Bear: sustained fall (−20% from a high). The likely origin is the animals' attack styles, plus an old proverb about selling the bearskin before catching the bear — betting on a fall.
March 2020's COVID crash was a three-month bear phase; the recovery after was a textbook bull run. 'Bullish on X' simply means expecting X to rise.

learn.title: Market cap, and why price ≠ size
learn.teaser: A ₹5,000 stock can be a smaller company than a ₹500 one.
body: Market capitalisation = share price × number of shares. MRF's famous ₹1+ lakh share price makes it nowhere near India's biggest company; Reliance's much cheaper shares multiply across far more of them.

Caps define categories — large (top 100), mid (101–250), small (rest) by SEBI's rules — which matters because mutual funds are mandated to buy within their category. A stock crossing a cap boundary mechanically gains or loses buyers.

learn.title: Percentiles: how analysts avoid lying
learn.teaser: p50, p90, p99 — the shape of reality, not one number.
body: An average API response of 200ms hides that 1% of users wait 4 seconds. p50 (median) shows the typical case, p90 the bad day, p99 the disaster tail. Same for delivery times, load times, ticket resolution.
Reporting reflex: any latency, wait-time or money metric deserves at least p50 + p90. If someone gives you only the mean, they're either new or hiding the tail.

learn.title: The 1962 war India doesn't celebrate
learn.teaser: A month-long border war with China reshaped Indian defence policy.
body: In October 1962 Chinese forces advanced across the disputed Himalayan border (Aksai Chin, NEFA — today's Arunachal), overwhelming underprepared Indian positions before declaring a unilateral ceasefire. Nehru's reputation never fully recovered.
The war triggered India's military modernisation and its tilt toward Soviet arms, and the unresolved Line of Actual Control remains the flashpoint behind standoffs like Galwan 2020.
